heading: TONUS QUARTUS
card_summary: This chunk opens the Fourth Tone of De Harmonia Mundi with Chapter 1, titled "De convenientia quadam occulta rerum inter se, unde altera in alteram mirabiliter operatur" (On a certain hidden agreement among things, by which one acts marvelously on another). Invoking Hermes Trismegistus, Zorzi argues that God fashioned two images of himself — the world and man — in the first of which he plays and in the second takes delight. The chapter catalogs the occult sympathies (virtutes occultae) embedded in natural things: the magnet attracting iron, amber drawing chaff, the aetites stone aiding childbirth, jasper staunching blood, the remora halting ships, coral bringing joy, hyacinth strengthening travelers, and rhubarb purging bile. Zorzi argues that none of the natural philosophers who fill great volumes on the properties of things have explained the causes of these hidden virtues, which belong to a divine operative power revealed only to God's friends (amicis suis).
essay:
## Overview and Chapter Title

Chunk 92 opens the **Tonus Quartus** (Fourth Tone) of Francesco Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* with the first chapter: **"De convenientia quadam occulta rerum inter se, unde altera in alteram mirabiliter operatur"** — "On a certain hidden agreement among things, by which one acts marvelously on another." The chapter heading encapsulates the dominant concern of this section: the doctrine of **occult sympathy** (*convenientia occulta*), by which disparate natural entities act upon one another through hidden, non-mechanical bonds. This is a foundational topic in Renaissance natural philosophy, linking Zorzi's Kabbalistic and Neoplatonic synthesis to the tradition of *magia naturalis*.

## The Hermetic Frame: Two Images of God

Zorzi opens by invoking **Hermes Trismegistus** — the legendary Egyptian sage whose *Corpus Hermeticum* was central to Renaissance syncretist thought — to establish the theological frame:

> *Exuperantissimus opifex Deus finxit (ut Trismegistus ait) duas sui imagines, mundum videlicet atque hominem, in quorum altero luderet, in altero vero delitiis frueretur.*

<cite>p. 2206</cite>

"The most excellent craftsman God fashioned (as Trismegistus says) two images of himself, namely the world and man, in one of which he might play, and in the other truly take delight." The distinction between *ludere* (to play) in the world and *delitiis frui* (to enjoy delights) in man encodes the Neoplatonic hierarchy: the cosmos is the theater of divine play, but the human being — as microcosm — is the site of divine intimacy and participation. This sets up the macrocosm-microcosm analogy that structures the entire *De Harmonia Mundi*.

## Occult Operations: Beyond Qualitative, Elemental, and Stellar Causation

Zorzi then specifies the mode of divine operation in the world. God does not merely act through *qualitativa*, *elementaris*, or *sydereal* (*syderea*) virtue — the three standard categories of scholastic and Aristotelian natural philosophy — but through a higher principle:

> *vi quadam ingeniis hominum occulta, nisi quatenus ipse amicis suis manifestare dignatur.*

<cite>p. 2206</cite>

"...a certain force hidden from human minds, except insofar as he himself deigns to reveal it to his friends." The phrase *amicis suis* — "to his friends" — points toward the elect, the initiated, those to whom divine wisdom is specially communicated. This is consistent with Zorzi's broader Kabbalistic commitments, where esoteric knowledge is transmitted through a chain of initiated receivers (*mekubbalim*). The passage thus frames occult natural philosophy as a species of revealed theology: the hidden causes in nature are not accessible by ordinary intellect but only by divine favor.

## The Catalog of Occult Sympathies

The bulk of the passage is a catalogue of natural objects exhibiting *virtutes occultae* — hidden properties whose causes remain unknown to natural philosophers even in their "great volumes" (*ingentia volumina*). Zorzi lists:

- **The magnet** (*magnes*): attracts iron — universally known yet causally unexplained
- **Amber** (*electrum*) and **balas ruby** (*balagius*): draw chaff when warmed by friction
- **Eagle stone** (*aetites*): placed on pregnant women, strengthens the fetus; placed beneath, draws it out
- **Jasper** (*iaspis*): staunches blood
- **The remora** (*echinus* / *remora*): halts ships — one of the most celebrated marvels of ancient natural history, derived from Pliny
- **Coral** (*corallus*): when worn, produces a certain gladness (*laetitia*) in the person
- **Hyacinth stone** (*hyacinthus*): an excellent companion for travelers, conferring strength
- **Rhubarb** (*rheu barbarum*, called *ravend* by the Arabs): expels choler from the human body
- Various medicinal substances that draw off and expel diverse humors

> *Sic nec unde habeat aetites, ut foetum mulierum, et plantarum superposita corroboret, et supposita trahat. Nec unde iaspis sanguinem comprimat, et echneis (quae et remora dicitur) navem sistat.*

<cite>p. 2206–2207</cite>

Zorzi's point is not to explain these phenomena but to insist on their inexplicability within standard natural philosophy — their causes belong to a hidden register that only the *convenientia occulta* can account for.

## The Rooster and Animals with Prophetic Powers

The chapter closes by extending the catalog to the animal kingdom. Zorzi notes that many animals possess a hidden power that is *praestigiosa atque fatidica* — "wondrous and prophetic":

> *Gallus cantu horas solertissime indicat, et alis apertis leonem fugat.*

<cite>p. 2207</cite>

"The rooster most skillfully indicates the hours by its crowing, and with spread wings puts the lion to flight." This famous *admirabile* — that a rooster can rout a lion — comes from ancient and medieval bestiaries and natural historical tradition. It exemplifies Zorzi's method: a seemingly impossible natural fact points to the operation of hidden sympathetic and antipathetic forces that transcend ordinary causation.

## Significance within the Larger Project

This opening chapter of Tonus Quartus establishes the theoretical ground for what follows: a systematic account of how harmony operates not only in musical and mathematical registers but in the hidden sympathies and antipathies of the natural world. The Hermetic citation from Trismegistus, the explicit critique of Aristotelian categories (qualitative, elemental, stellar), and the appeal to divine favor as the condition for knowing occult causes all place Zorzi firmly within the Ficinian and Piconian synthesis of Hermeticism, Kabbalah, and Neoplatonism. The Italian section header — "E L'UOMO BENE ARMONIZZATO AFFINCHÉ POSSA" ("And the well-harmonized man so that he can...") — signals that the Tonus will move toward the human being's role as the privileged locus of divine harmony, the second divine image in which God takes delight rather than merely plays.
